title: Lagging cold water pipes
subtitle: Slide on the foam tube, secure at bends, seal the joins
excerpt: How to lag cold water pipes with foam tube insulation to prevent freezing: measuring and cutting the correct size insulation, fitting around bends and fittings, and securing with adhesive tape or cable ties.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Energy Saving Trust guidance on pipe insulation (publicly available). BS 5970 specification for pipe insulation.

glossaryTerms.definition: Closed-cell foam tube, slit along one side, sized to fit snugly over standard copper or plastic pipe. Available in 15 mm, 22 mm, and 28 mm bore sizes to match standard pipe diameters. Wall thickness is 9 mm standard; 13 mm for exposed or very cold locations.

content.text: Cold water pipes in unheated spaces (loft, garage, under-floor voids, external wall runs) are vulnerable to freezing in cold winters. A burst pipe in a loft can release hundreds of litres before it is discovered. Fitting

reduces the rate at which a pipe loses heat to the surrounding air, extending the time before the water inside freezes. It does not prevent freezing indefinitely in extreme cold; the pipe still needs to have a path to a heated space.

Measure the outside diameter of the pipe. A 15 mm copper pipe is 15 mm outside diameter; a 22 mm copper pipe is 22 mm. Buy the matching bore foam. When in doubt, buy the slightly larger bore; it will not grip as firmly but will still insulate.
Self-adhesive foil tape for sealing the slit join and joining lengths end to end.
For cutting the foam tube to length and for mitring around bends.
Step 1, open and slide the foam tube.
The foam tube has a pre-slit along its length. Open the slit and press the foam onto the pipe, running it along the pipe until the slit closes. The foam should grip the pipe firmly.
Press a strip of self-adhesive foam tape along the slit join for the full length of the run. Also tape the end joins between lengths, ensuring no gap in the insulation.

At a 90-degree bend, cut each piece of foam at 45 degrees at the elbow point. The two 45-degree cut faces press together to form the corner. If the bend is a long-radius sweep rather than a sharp elbow, cut small V-shaped notches in the slit side of the foam at intervals so the tube can flex around the bend without splitting.

At valves, compression fittings, or other bulkhead items, cut the foam short to the edge of the fitting. Wrap exposed fittings that are vulnerable to frost with foam insulation tape, which is more flexible and easier to fit around irregular shapes than tube insulation.

attrs.cause: The foam thickness is insufficient for the air temperature in the location, or there are gaps in the lagging at joints or fittings.
attrs.fix: Use 13 mm wall-thickness lagging for very cold loft or external locations. Check every joint and fitting for gaps and seal with foam tape.

attrs.symptom: Foam lagging won't stay closed at the slit
attrs.cause: Bore size is too large for the pipe, or the adhesive tape was not applied.
attrs.fix: Apply self-adhesive foam tape along the full length of the slit before the foam springs open. Use cable ties at 300 mm centres as a backup.
